name: Ubayy ibn Ka'b
title: Ubayy ibn Ka'b (RA): The Master of the Reciters
meta_description: The story of Ubayy ibn Ka'b (RA), the companion the Prophet Muhammad called the master of the reciters. The scribe of revelation, the imam of Madinah, and the man Allah named by name.
keywords: Ubayy ibn Ka'b, story of Ubayy ibn Kab, master of the reciters, scribe of revelation, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories, people of the Quran
lede: If you had walked into the Prophet's mosque in Madinah a few years after he ﷺ had passed away, you would have found one man at the center of almost every scene. Leading the prayer. Pacing the floor reciting quietly to himself. Sitting with people who had come to ask about a verse.

This is the story of Ubayy ibn Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him): the scribe who caught revelation while it was still fresh, the man the Prophet ﷺ called the best reciter of his nation, and the teacher whose chain still links almost every reciter of the Quran back to the Prophet himself.

sections.h2: A man who could already read
paras: Ubayy was from the Khazraj of Madinah, from the sub-tribe of Banu al-Najjar, the maternal relatives of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He was one of the few among his people who could read and write before Islam, in an age when most could not.

He was older than the other young scribes around him, close in age to the Prophet ﷺ himself. By the time he died, those who described him remembered a man who seemed to stand out in every detail: exceedingly white hair, an exceedingly white beard, bright skin, and deep black eyes. They said there was not a single dark hair left on his head or his beard.

He accepted Islam early, then traveled to Makkah and took the pledge with the Prophet ﷺ. When the Prophet ﷺ reached Madinah, Ubayy was the first of the Ansar to write down the revelation for him. The Quran you hold today was preserved through hands like his.

The Prophet ﷺ once told his companions to take the Quran from four people, and Ubayy was one of them. Three of those four were Ansar known for the Quran, and people rarely mention them apart from one another.

Each of the three had a distinction. One was the best in understanding meaning and jurisprudence. One was the best in the laws of inheritance. But of the recitation itself, the Prophet ﷺ singled out Ubayy: the best of my nation in reciting the Quran is Ubayy.
This was not a small thing to say among such men. He was the oldest of the three and, in the words of the Prophet ﷺ, the greatest of them in recitation. The honor was specific, and it was his.

The Prophet ﷺ honored him in other ways too, sometimes calling him the master, or chief, of the Ansar. When revelation came down, he was among those the Prophet ﷺ called to capture the words at once. So much of what we know about how the Prophet ﷺ recited, and even the backstory of how certain surahs came to be revealed, reaches us through Ubayy.

sections.h2: The day Allah said his name
paras: The Prophet ﷺ once came to him and asked directly: which verse of what you have memorized is the greatest? Ubayy answered at once, reciting Ayat al-Kursi. The Prophet ﷺ stepped toward him, placed a hand on his chest, and told him that knowledge would now come to him with ease. Years later, Ubayy could still recall the weight of that hand.

On another day the Prophet ﷺ told him something that stopped him in his place: Allah had commanded the Prophet ﷺ to recite the Quran to Ubayy. Ubayy was stunned. Allah named me to you, by name? he asked. Out of all people, me? The Prophet ﷺ confirmed it: Allah had mentioned him by name and lineage in the presence of the angels closest to Him.

Ubayy began to weep. Of everything he carried from his years with the Prophet ﷺ, no moment was more precious. He was a serious man, not given to easy joy, yet his son said that whenever his father told this story, he could not hide his happiness. When asked why, Ubayy answered with the words of the Quran:

ayah.text: Say [Prophet], 'In God's grace and mercy let them rejoice: these are better than all they accumulate.'

paras: Ubayy belonged to a small group of companions known for the Quran, and they were so close that the seerah rarely mentions them apart from one another. When the Prophet's grandson lay dying, his daughter sent for him, and the messenger found the Prophet ﷺ seated with these three men. They were a trio, and when the child passed, it was these three the Prophet ﷺ took with him.

Ubayy described his own nearness to the Quran in a way no one else could. He used to say that he received the recitation from the one who received it from the angel, while it was still fresh. He was there as the words came down.

His life with the Quran was quiet and constant. He reviewed the entire Quran over eight nights, a pace the companions considered taking your time with it. He was almost always in worship, and when people came with a question, he would set his recitation aside, sit with them, answer, and then return to it.

If you had looked for him outside the mosque, you would have struggled to find a single story. Nearly every account of Ubayy places him inside the mosque of Madinah: leading the prayer, pacing as he recited to himself, or teaching those who gathered around him.

sections.h2: The chain that runs through him
paras: There is something almost every student of the Quran shares without knowing his name. When a reciter reads to a teacher, who read to a teacher before him, the chain of teachers reaching back across the centuries almost always passes through Ubayy ibn Ka'b on its way to the Prophet ﷺ.

Of the ten famous schools of recitation, nine trace their link to the Prophet ﷺ through Ubayy. Only one does not. He is the quiet bridge between a child sounding out a verse today and the man who first heard it fresh.

He was also the agreed imam of the companions in prayer. When the night prayers of Ramadan were scattered behind different leaders, it was Ubayy who was chosen to gather everyone behind one voice. If you imagined the mosque of Madinah in those years, the man leading was him.

His knowledge of the Quran reached into its smallest details. When people pushed themselves harder on the twenty-seventh night of Ramadan in search of the Night of Decree, it traced back to Ubayy, who held that it fell on that night. Umar himself said that for any question about the Quran, the people should go to Ubayy.

sections.h2: Pure for the Quran, not for power
paras: When Umar (RA) led the Muslims, he appointed many companions as judges and governors, but never Ubayy. Ubayy noticed, and gently asked why. Umar's answer was tender and exact: I was afraid it would stain your religion. I did not want this to take anything from you.

Ubayy had a strict heart. The growing wealth and competition he saw around him troubled him deeply, and he wanted people to return to the Quran. Yet he also understood balance. When a man once stood before Umar and belittled this world until it sounded worthless, it was Ubayy who corrected him: this world holds our provision for the next, and the good deeds we can be rewarded for. The world is not the enemy. It is the opportunity.
He wept when he recited the verse warning of punishment and division, and he asked Allah to spare him from seeing the believers turn against one another.

ayah.text: Say, 'He has power to send punishment on you from above or from under your very feet, or to divide you into discordant factions and make some taste the violence of others.' See how We explain Our revelation in various ways, so that they may understand,

sections.h2: The streets that filled for him
paras: Ubayy asked Allah for something most of us would never dare to ask. He asked for a constant fever, so he could carry its reward, but on one condition: that it never keep him from the mosque, from Hajj, or from setting out in the path of Allah. The narrations say it was granted in exactly that way. The fever stayed with him, and lifted whenever it was time to worship.

He was spared the trials he had feared so much. He died before discord broke out among the Muslims. When he passed, the people of Madinah poured into the streets. A visitor from Iraq, seeing the crowds, asked what was happening, and was told: you must not be from here. Today the master of the Muslims has died.

His last counsel was the Quran itself. To a man who asked him for advice, he said: take the Book of Allah as your guide and be content with it as your judge. It is your intercessor, and there is no doubt in it. Then he warned, in the words of the Quran, of the day people would be found to have neglected it. He was buried near the mosque of the Prophet ﷺ, among the companions, the imam of the reciters laid to rest in the city he had filled with recitation.

lessons_intro: His greatness was not in command or conquest. It was in a lifelong closeness to one thing, held quietly, every single day.

lessons.bold: Master one thing with your whole heart.
lessons.text: Ubayy was not the judge or the governor. He was the reciter, completely, and that devotion outlived every title around him.

lessons.bold: Stay close to the words of Allah.
lessons.text: He paced the mosque reciting, reviewed the whole Quran over eight nights, and answered every question from it. Nearness to the Book was the shape of his day.

lessons.bold: The world is an opportunity, not an enemy.
lessons.text: Even with his strict heart, he refused to call this life worthless. It carries our provision for the next, and the good we can still do.

lessons.bold: Honor is not the same as position.
lessons.text: He was never given power, yet he was called the master of the Muslims. Where you stand with Allah is not measured by your rank among people.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah, among the first to write down the revelation. The Prophet ﷺ called him the best reciter of his nation, and he is remembered as the master of the reciters.

faq.q: Why is Ubayy ibn Ka'b called the master of the reciters?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ named him the best of his nation in reciting the Quran, and asked Ubayy to recite to him so he could listen. Almost every chain of Quran recitation today traces back to the Prophet ﷺ through Ubayy.

faq.q: What is special about Allah mentioning Ubayy by name?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ told him that Allah had commanded him to recite the Quran to Ubayy, naming him by name and lineage before the angels. Ubayy considered it the most precious moment of his life and wept when he recalled it.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Ubayy ibn Ka'b?
faq.a: To master one good thing with the whole heart, to keep close to the words of Allah, and to understand that honor with Allah is not the same as position among people.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Ubayy never led an army or governed a province. He gave himself to one thing, the Quran, and held it so closely that centuries later his name still sits quietly in the chain behind almost every person who recites. He was offered nothing the world counts as power, and was given something far rarer: to be named, and loved, for what he gave his days to. His life is a reminder that depth can matter more than reach.

Most of us will not be famous, and will never be put in charge of much. Ubayy's life asks a gentler question: is there one good thing you love enough to return to every single day? Choose it, stay near it, and let it shape your hours the way the Quran shaped his. That is a life that quietly leaves a mark, long after the noise has faded.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Ubayy ibn Ka'b (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Quran translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 10:58 and 6:65). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:

Pacing, reciting quietly to himself.

Answering every question about the Quran.

That was Ubayy ibn Ka'b.

He was one of the few who could read and write before Islam.

When the Prophet ﷺ reached Madinah, Ubayy became the first of the Ansar to write down the revelation.

The Prophet ﷺ said: take the Quran from four people.

Ubayy was one of them.

And of recitation, the Prophet ﷺ named him **the best of my nation**.

One day the Prophet ﷺ told him:

"Allah has commanded me to recite the Quran to you."

Ubayy was stunned. "Allah named me? By name? Me?"

Then he wept.

He said no moment with the Prophet ﷺ was more precious.

A serious man, not given to joy.

But whenever he told this story, he could not hide his happiness.

Almost every chain of Quran recitation today runs back to the Prophet ﷺ **through Ubayy**.

The quiet bridge between a child sounding out a verse and the words heard fresh.

Umar never made him a governor.

Ubayy asked why. Umar said:

"I was afraid it would stain your religion. I did not want this to take anything from you."

**Honor is not the same as position.**

He held no power. Yet when he died, the streets of Madinah filled, and they called him the master of the Muslims.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
He never led an army or governed a province. Ubayy ibn Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him) gave himself to one thing: the Quran.

He was among the first to write down the revelation, and the Prophet ﷺ called him the best reciter of his nation. When the Prophet ﷺ told him that Allah had named him by name and commanded that the Quran be recited to him, Ubayy wept. He said no moment was more precious to him than that.

Almost every chain of recitation today still runs back to the Prophet ﷺ through Ubayy. Depth, held quietly, can outlast every louder thing.

Which part of his story stayed with you?

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/ubayy-ibn-kab

email.subject: The companion Allah named by name
email.preview: He was the first of the Ansar to write down revelation, and the best reciter of his nation.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some of the companions are remembered for battles, others for leadership. Ubayy ibn Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him) is remembered for one thing held with his whole heart: the Quran.

He was among the few in Madinah who could read and write before Islam, and when the Prophet ﷺ arrived, Ubayy became the first of the Ansar to write down the revelation. The Prophet ﷺ told his companions to take the Quran from four people, and Ubayy was one of them. Of the recitation itself, the Prophet ﷺ called him the best of his nation.

Then came the moment Ubayy treasured above all others. The Prophet ﷺ told him that Allah had commanded him to recite the Quran to Ubayy, naming him by name before the angels. Ubayy was stunned. "Allah mentioned me? By name? Me?" he asked. And then he wept.

He was a serious man, not someone given to easy joy. But his son said that whenever his father told this story, the happiness on his face could not be hidden.

His closeness to the Quran was not for a single moment. He reviewed the whole of it every eight nights, and almost every account of him places him inside the mosque of Madinah, leading the prayer or quietly reciting or answering questions about a verse. Across the centuries, nearly every chain of recitation still runs back to the Prophet ﷺ through Ubayy.

There is a quieter lesson in his life too. When Umar (RA) led the Muslims, he appointed many companions as judges and governors, but never Ubayy. When Ubayy asked why, Umar answered gently: "I was afraid it would stain your religion. I did not want this to take anything from you."

Ubayy held no rank. Yet when he died, the people of Madinah flooded the streets, and a visitor was told: today the master of the Muslims has passed away. Honor with Allah is not the same as position among people.

His life asks us a gentle question: is there one good thing you love enough to return to every single day? He chose the Quran, stayed near it, and let it shape his hours.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: If you had walked into the Prophet's mosque in Madinah a few years after he ﷺ had passed away, you would have found one man at the center of almost every scene: leading the prayer, pacing the floor reciting quietly, sitting with those who came to ask about a verse. That was Ubayy ibn Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him).

He was from the Ansar of Madinah, one of the few who could read and write before Islam. When the Prophet ﷺ arrived in the city, Ubayy became the first of the Ansar to write down the revelation. The Quran we hold today was preserved through hands like his.

The Prophet ﷺ told his companions to take the Quran from four people, and Ubayy was one. Of the recitation itself, the Prophet ﷺ called him the best of his nation. One day the Prophet ﷺ even placed a hand on Ubayy's chest and told him that knowledge would now come to him with ease, a moment Ubayy could still feel years later.

Then came the moment he treasured most. The Prophet ﷺ told him that Allah had commanded that the Quran be recited to him, naming Ubayy by name before the angels. Ubayy was stunned. "Allah mentioned me? By name? Me?" he asked, and then he wept. A serious man, not given to joy, he could never hide his happiness when he told this story.

Almost every chain of Quran recitation across the centuries still runs back to the Prophet ﷺ through Ubayy. When the night prayers of Ramadan were scattered, it was Ubayy who was chosen to gather everyone behind one voice. He was the imam of the reciters.

Yet Umar never gave him power. When Ubayy asked why, Umar said gently, "I was afraid it would stain your religion." Ubayy held no rank, and still, when he died, the streets of Madinah filled, and they called him the master of the Muslims.
His final advice was simple: take the Book of Allah as your guide and be content with it. He was buried among the companions, near the mosque he had filled with recitation.
storybook.pullquote: Allah mentioned me? By name? Me?
storybook.reflection: Ubayy never led an army or held office, yet his name sits quietly in the chain behind almost every reciter who ever lived. His life asks a gentle question: is there one good thing you love enough to return to every single day? Choose it, stay near it, and let it shape your hours. Depth held quietly can outlast every louder thing.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
